elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the evolution of supernova remnants (SNRs) in a two-phase cloudy medium by performing a series of high-resolution (up to $\Delta x\approx0.01\,\mathrm{pc}$), 3D hydrodynamical simulations including radiative cooling and thermal conduction.
elements.sent-2411-002: We aim to reach a resolution that directly captures the shock-cloud interactions for the majority of the clouds initialized by the saturation of thermal instability.
elements.sent-2411-003: In comparison to the SNR in a uniform medium with the volume filling warm medium, the SNR expands similarly (following $\propto t^{2/5}$) but sweeps up more mass as the cold clouds contribute before shocks in the warm medium become radiative.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, the SNR in a cloudy medium continuously loses energy after shocks toward the cold clouds cool, resulting in less hot gas mass, thermal energy, and terminal momentum.
elements.sent-2411-005: Thermal conduction has little effect on the dynamics of the SNR but smooths the morphology and modifies the internal structure by increasing the density of hot gas by a factor of $\sim 3-5$.
elements.sent-2411-006: The simulation results are not fully consistent with many previous 1D models describing the SNR in a cloudy medium including a mass loading term.
elements.sent-2411-007: By direct measurement in the simulations, we find that, apart from the mass source, the energy sink is also important with a spatially flat cooling rate $\dot{e}\propto t^{-11/5}$.
elements.sent-2411-008: As an illustration, we show an example 1D model including both mass source and energy sink terms (in addition to the radiative cooling in the volume filling component) that better describes the structure of the simulated SNR.
elements.sent-2411-009: Supernova (SN) explosions are believed to be crucial in shaping interstellar medium (ISM), producing the hot phase, driving turbulence, regulating the star formation rate, and launching galactic outflows (see \citealt{Cox2005ARA A..43..337C for a review to classical views and \citealt{Kim2017ApJ...846..133K, Kim2020ApJ...900...61K, Kim2023ApJ...946....3K for a modern view).}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The explosion of SN ejects fast material with a typical mass of $M_\mathrm{ej}\sim 1$-$10\,\mathrm{M_\odot}$ and kinetic energy of $E_\mathrm{SN}\sim10^{51}\mathrm{erg}$ \citep{Draine2011piim.book.....D .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The localized injection of such high energy SN ejecta in turn launches blastwaves expanding into the surrounding medium.
elements.sent-2411-012: Earlier works have described the evolution of SNRs using spherically symmetric expansion in a uniform medium, which is often characterized into four distinct stages marked by the power law index of the spherical expansion \citep{Cox1972ApJ...178..159C, Woltjer1972ARA A..10..129W, Cioffi1988ApJ...334..252C, Ostriker McKee1988RvMP...60....1O, Draine2011piim.book.....D : free expansion stage ($r\propto t$), energy-conserving stage (so-called Sedov~\citep{Sedov1959sdmm.book.....S and Taylor~\citep{Taylor1950RSPSA.201..159T (ST) stage; $r\propto t^{2/5}$), pressure-driven snowplow (PDS) stage ($r\propto t^{2/7}$), and momentum-conserving snowplow stage ($r\propto t^{1/4}$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the ISM is far from being uniform, consisting of multiple distinct thermal phases over a wider range of temperature and density.
elements.sent-2411-014: SNe themselves are responsible for making the ISM multiphase, by shock heating to create the hot phase with $T\sim 10^{6-7}\,\mathrm{K}$ \citep{Cox Smith1974ApJ...189L.105C, McKee Ostriker1977ApJ...218..148M .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In addition, the ISM is also heated by UV radiation and cosmic ray ionization, providing the major heating in the neutral medium and maintaining warm neutral medium (WNM) at $T\sim 10^4\,\mathrm{K}$ and cold neutral medium (CNM) at $T\sim 10^2\,\mathrm{K}$ \citep{Field1965ApJ...142..531F, Field1969ApJ...155L.149F, Wolfire1995ApJ...443..152W .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The molecular gas and the photoionized gas are significant constituents of the ISM, while they share similar thermal states with the CNM and WNM, respectively \citep{Kim2023ApJ...946....3K, Rathjen2021MNRAS.504.1039R .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The complexity is added by turbulence (also mainly driven by SNe), which broadens the density and temperature distributions from a single characteristic state \citep[e.g.,][]{Ostriker2001ApJ...546..980O and continuously populates thermally unstable (thought to be transient) states \citep{Gazol2013ApJ...765...49G, Ho2024arXiv240714199H .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: With the pervasive diffuse hot and warm gas filling most of the volume, the cold gas is confined in dense clouds occupying only $\sim 1\%$ of the volume of the local ISM\citep[e.g.,][]{Draine2011piim.book.....D, Kim2023ApJ...946....3K .}
elements.sent-2411-019: It is thus well motivated to consider the effect of clouds in the SNR evolution as ``impurities'' of the spherical blastwave expansions by incorporating the consequence of the shock-cloud interactions \citep[e.g.,][for a review]{Ostriker McKee1988RvMP...60....1O .}
elements.sent-2411-020: A plethora of dedicated studies on the shock-cloud interaction (there is a separate collection of the wind-cloud interaction literature) has been conducted starting from pure hydrodynamical simulations \citep[e.g.,][]{Bedogni1990A A...231..481B, Klein1994ApJ...420..213K, Pittard Parkin2016MNRAS.457.4470P to more complex problems including magnetic fields, thermal conduction (isotropic and anisotropic), turbulence, and radiative cooling \citep[e.g.,][to name a few]{MacLow1994ApJ...433..757M, Gregori1999ApJ...527L.113G, Shin2008ApJ...680..336S, Orlando2005A A...444..505O, Goldsmith2017MNRAS.470.2427G, 2020MNRAS.499.2173B .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The major effect often considered in such 1D impurity models is the mass loading due to cloud crushing and conductive evaporation.
elements.sent-2411-022: Different forms of mass loading prescriptions were considered in 1D time-dependent spherical simulations by assuming constant, Mach number dependent, and temperature-dependent mass loading rates \citep[e.g.,][]{Dyson2002A A...390.1063D, Pittard2003A A...401.1027P .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Recent work by \citet{Pittard2019MNRAS.488.3376P investigated the effect of constant mass loading rate and concluded that mass-loaded SNRs attain higher radial momentum and more hot gas at shell formation but lower final momentum due to more rapid cooling and less PdV work during the PDS stage.}
elements.sent-2411-024: While well motivated, the validation of the 1D impurity models requires direct global SNR simulations in a cloudy medium with high resolution to capture the major consequences of the shock-cloud interaction.
elements.sent-2411-025: Especially, the role of radiative cooling in the turbulent mixing layers \citep{Fielding2020ApJ...894L..24F, Tan2021MNRAS.502.3179T has recently been emphasized in the wind-cloud interaction literature (mostly in the galactic wind context), leading to qualitative changes in the results \citep{Armillotta2016MNRAS.462.4157A, Gronke2018MNRAS.480L.111G, Sparre2020MNRAS.499.4261S, Li2020MNRAS.492.1841L, Abruzzo2022ApJ...925..199A .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Namely, the interaction between the fast-moving hot gas with large clouds creates a turbulent mixing layer within which the radiative cooling is so strong that the surrounding hot gas condenses onto clouds.
elements.sent-2411-027: The net effect is then the growth of clouds with significant energy loss from the hot gas rather than simple shredding and evaporation of clouds.
elements.sent-2411-028: In a similar vein, stellar wind blown bubbles experience an efficient cooling when expanding into a turbulent medium, leading to the ``momentum-conserving''-like solution with a minimal enhancement of momentum compared to the injected wind momentum \citep{Lancaster2021ApJ...914...89L, Lancaster2021ApJ...914...90L .}
elements.sent-2411-029: It is thus important to access previous approaches using direct 3D simulations that are capable of capturing radiative cooling at the shock-cloud interaction layers.
elements.sent-2411-030: Recent advances in hardware and software in computational astrophysics make direct 3D numerical simulations of radiative SNRs in a cloudy medium possible.
elements.sent-2411-031: About a decade ago, a number of such simulations are performed with different realizations of cloudy medium: placing cold clouds manually ~\citep{Li2015ApJ...814....4L, Slavin2017ApJ...846...77S , turbulent molecular clouds~\citep{Walch2015MNRAS.451.2757W, Iffrig2015A A...576A..95I , Gaussian random field realization using log-normal density distribution \citep{Martizzi2015MNRAS.450..504M, Zhang2019MNRAS.482.1602Z , and a saturated state of thermal instability \citep{Kim2015ApJ...802...99K, Kim2017ApJ...834...25K .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: One of the main results in these simulations is that the characteristic properties of SNRs including the mass and momentum at the shell formation are still well approximated by the classical 1D results when using the mean density of the surrounding medium.
elements.sent-2411-033: These simulation results neither follow the SNR expansion solution modified by cloud evaporation \citep{McKee Ostriker1977ApJ...218..148M nor show significant enhancements in momentum injection before and at shell formation when a certain mass loading model is assumed \citep{Pittard2019MNRAS.488.3376P .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, it is still unclear whether the discrepancies between 3D simulations and 1D impurity models are due to insufficient numerical resolution in the simulations to fully capture the shock-cloud interaction.
elements.sent-2411-035: On the one hand, one needs a minimal resolution of 32-64 cells per radius for a single cloud to resolve the hydrodynamical interaction and cloud ablation \citep{Pittard Parkin2016MNRAS.457.4470P .}
elements.sent-2411-036: On the other hand, although the mass and energy exchanges in the turbulent mixing layer formally require much higher resolution (e.g., resolving the turbulent cascade down to cooling and conductive length scales), apparent convergence can achieved at lower resolutions than the formal requirements if the largest scale for turbulence is resolved \citep[e.g.,][]{Fielding2020ApJ...894L..24F, Tan2021MNRAS.502.3179T, Lancaster2024ApJ...970...18L .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Another technological advance in the last decade is represented by the deployment of GPU-accelerated high-performance computers.
elements.sent-2411-038: When utilizing such capabilities, direct 3D simulations with about an order of magnitude higher spatial resolution are easily accessible.
elements.sent-2411-039: At the same time, adopting higher-order methods allows even higher-fidelity simulations.
elements.sent-2411-040: We use a newly developed performance portable magnetohydrodynamic code \athenak{ \citep{Stone2024arXiv240916053S and run a series of hydrodynamical simulations of radiative SNRs in a two-phase cloudy medium (a setup similar to \citealt{Kim2015ApJ...802...99K ) at varying resolution with and without thermal conduction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The highest resolution we achieve is $1/64$ pc while we follow the global SNR evolution until the forward shock toward the WNM begins to cool at the age of $\sim 30$ kyr and radius of $\sim 20$ pc.
elements.sent-2411-042: Although our resolution is not sufficient to reach a formal resolution requirement for cooling and conductive length scales, it is high enough to resolve hydrodynamic instabilities for the majority of clouds (down to cloud size of $0.5$ pc).
elements.sent-2411-043: In this work, we consider a condition with the ambient mean density of $n=10\,{\rm cm^{-3 }$ while we explore the effect of different realizations of a cloudy medium.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We focus on the effect of cold clouds in the evolution and internal structure of the volume-filling component (i.e., the hot gas) before the shell formation in the WNM (i.e., cooling of outer shocks propagating into the WNM).
elements.sent-2411-045: This is a nominal energy-conserving stage during which the hot gas mass and radial momentum increase significantly such that the dynamical impact of SNRs is determined.
elements.sent-2411-046: We delve into the roles of the shock-cloud interaction in terms of mass loading into the hot interior by cloud ablation (including conductive evaporation) and energy loss at cloud interfaces by the development of turbulent mixing layers.
elements.sent-2411-047: Finally, we present a minimal 1D model that reproduces the 3D simulation results.
elements.sent-2411-048: We emphasize that the set of simulations presented in this work only utilizes about $1.2\times10^4$ GPU hours ($\sim 2.4\times10^3$ GPU hours for each of the highest resolution runs), implying more systematic studies are possible to motivate the development of sophisticated models.

elements.section-2411-002: Summary of 1D, spherical SNR expansion theory
elements.sent-2411-053: As a reference throughout the paper, it is useful to summarize a standard theory of 1D, spherically-symmetric SNR evolution in a uniform medium.
elements.sent-2411-054: The early theories identified four stages of evolution in which SNRs follow different expansion laws: free expansion stage ($r\propto t$), adiabatic expansion stage ($r\propto t^{2/5}$), pressure-driven snowplow (PDS) stage ($r\propto t^{2/7}$), and momentum-conserving snowplow stage ($r\propto t^{1/4}$).
elements.sent-2411-055: During the free expansion stage when the SN ejecta expands ballistically, the SNR sweeps up more and more material.
elements.sent-2411-056: In doing so, reverse shocks develop and thermalize the interior, entering the so-called Sedov~\citep{Sedov1959sdmm.book.....S and Taylor~\citep{Taylor1950RSPSA.201..159T (ST) stage, a main focus of this work.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: In the ST stage, the explosion is well described by an adiabatic point source explosion, which is solely determined by the total energy $E_\mathrm{SN}$ and the ambient density $\rho_0$.
elements.sent-2411-058: A self-similar solution can be obtained \citep[e.g.,][]{Sedov1959sdmm.book.....S, Draine2011piim.book.....D to give (for an adiabatic index $\gamma=5/3$) \begin{equation}r_S =5.88\,pc\,E_{51}^{1/5}n_0^{-1/5}t_3^{2/5},\label{eq:r_s} v_S =\frac{2}{5}\frac{r_S}{t}=2.35\times10^3\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}\,E_{51}^{1/5}n_0^{-1/5}t_3^{-3/5},\label{eq:v_s} T_S =\frac{3}{16}\frac{\mu v_S^2}{k_B}=7.37\times10^7\,K\,E_{51}^{2/5}n_0^{-2/5}t_3^{-6/5},\label{eq:T_s}\end{equation} where $E_{51 =E_\mathrm{SN /10^{51}\,\mathrm{erg}$ is the supernova expansion energy, $n_0=n/1\,\mathrm{cm^{-3 }$ is the total number density, and the mean molecular weight $\mu=0.618$ is adopted for fully ionized gas.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The ST stage ends when the radiative energy losses are no longer negligible.
elements.sent-2411-060: The postshock gas experiences rapid cooling and forms a dense shell.
elements.sent-2411-061: Using an approximation of cooling function for the postshock gas temperature range, $\Lambda(T) = 3.0\times10^{-22 {\rm erg\,s^{-1}\,cm^{3 (T/10^6\,\mathrm{K )^{-0.7}$ \citep[slight higher than][but appropriate for our adopted cooling function]{Draine2011piim.book.....D , one can obtain the shell formation time\footnote{We adopt the definition presented in \citet{Kim2015ApJ...802...99K .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Plugging this into \eqs\ref{eq:r_s and \ref{eq:v_s , we have the size and expansion velocity at the shell formation, respectively, \begin{equation}r_sf =22.9\,pc\,E_51^{0.29}n_0^{-0.42}, v_sf =299\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}\,E_51^{0.07}n_0^{0.13}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: It is worth emphasizing that the ST stage is the most dynamically important stage of SNR evolution during which SNRs build up both hot gas mass and radial momentum significantly.
elements.sent-2411-064: At the end of the ST stage, the hot gas mass (or swept up mass) and total radial momentum reach \begin{equation}M_{\rm sf} = 765\,M_\odot\,E_51^{0.87}n_0^{-0.26}, p_{\rm sf} = 1.47\times10^5, \,M_\odot\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}\,E_51^{0.94}n_0^{-0.13},\end{equation} which are more than 100 and 10 times larger than the ejecta mass and momentum, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-065: Our work focuses mainly on the detailed evolution of this stage.
elements.sent-2411-066: As references for the analysis presented in upcoming sections, we define a reference rate of change for mass, energy, density, and energy density, \begin{equation}\dot{M}_S\equiv{4\pi}\rho_0 v_S r_S^2, \dot{E}_S\equiv{4\pi}\rho_0 v_S^3 r_S^2, \dot{\rho}_S\equiv3\rho_0 v_S /r_S, \dot{e}_S\equiv3\rho_0 v_S^3 /r_S,\end{equation} as well as post shock density and pressure $\rho_\mathrm{S =4\rho_0$, $P_\mathrm{S =3/4\rho_0v_\mathrm{S ^2$ assuming strong shock.}}}

elements.sent-2411-117: To ensure the stability of the numerical results, we utilize the first-order flux correction (FOFC) algorithm as described by \citet{Lemaster Stone2009ApJ...691.1092L .}
elements.sent-2411-118: In this algorithm, we first identify the problematic cells in which any of the density, temperature, or pressure is smaller than its floor after the full hydro update but before source terms.
elements.sent-2411-119: Then we roll back, replace the higher-order numerical fluxes at the interface of the problematic cells with more diffusive spatially first-order fluxes computed using the Local Lax Friedrichs (LLF) solver, and update the cells.

elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_evo_fidu.pdf} \caption{Evolution of radius, mass, energy, and radial momentum of supernova remnants in the cloudy inhomogeneous background without (purple) and with (orange) thermal conduction. elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_rad.pdf} \caption{Radial profiles of hot gas density (top left), hot gas pressure (middle left), hot gas temperature (bottom left), total cooling rate (top right), hot gas cooling rate (middle right), and hot gas volume fraction (bottom right) at 10 kyr. elements.sent-2411-311: The main conclusions are summarized below.
elements.sent-2411-312: The cloud ablation and evaporation do not alter the expansion law in the SNR evolution, especially the ST stage.
elements.sent-2411-314: Mass loading has been incorporated in previous effective models, \citep[e.g.,][]{White Long1991ApJ...373..543W, Pittard2019MNRAS.488.3376P .}
elements.sent-2411-315: Here we find that the mass loading rate evolves with time with $\dot{\rho}\propto \dot{\rho _\mathrm{S}\propto t^{-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-316: In addition, the energy sink is also important with a spatially flat cooling rate $\dot{e}\propto \dot{e _\mathrm{S}\propto t^{-11/5}$.}
elements.sent-2411-317: This motivates that a more realistic 1D model should include both mass loading and energy sink as source terms.
elements.sent-2411-318: As an illustration, we construct a minimal 1D model including both terms and show that it can qualitatively describe the evolution and internal structure of the SNR.
elements.sent-2411-319: Thermal conduction does not drastically change the overall expansion law and evolution of key global properties.
elements.sent-2411-320: However, observational properties of SNRs can be affected significantly by thermal conduction.
elements.sent-2411-321: The inclusion of thermal conduction smooths the internal structure of the hot gas, increases the density of hot gas in the central region increases significantly, and decreases the temperature.
elements.sent-2411-322: This leads to increased cooling rates, especially in regions surrounding the cold clouds and within the turbulent mixing zones.
elements.sent-2411-323: However, even though our treatment of thermal conduction is maximal as we neglect magnetic fields and anisotropic conduction, we note that the total cooling rate is still dominated by turbulent mixing.
elements.sent-2411-324: Additional conductive evaporation from cold clouds increases the contribution of the cold gas component to the hot gas phase.
elements.sent-2411-325: The modeling of X-ray from SNRs thus requires a careful treatment of thermal conduction.
elements.sent-2411-326: The results illustrate the complex internal structures of SNR, which is of particular interest due to its importance and uncertainty in constituting the third phase of ISM and determining the X-ray emission of the supernova remnants \citep{Zhang2019MNRAS.482.1602Z .}

elements.sent-2411-346: We can rewrite them using the form of the velocity and entropy equations: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(r^2 v\rho) =\dot{\rho}, \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial P}{\partial r} =\frac{\dot{\varrho}-\dot{\rho}v}{\rho}, \frac{P}{\gamma-1}\frac{1}{S}\frac{d S}{d t} =\dot{e}-\dot{\varrho}v+\dot{\rho}\left(\frac{1}{2}v^2-\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\frac{P}{\rho}\right),\end{equation} where $S\equiv P/\rho^\gamma$.

elements.sent-2411-347: To find a self-similarity solution, we make the following substitutions: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} P=P_sf(x), \rho=\rho_sg(x), v=v_sh(x), \dot{\rho}=\dot{\rho}_sk(x), \dot{\varrho}=\dot{\varrho}_s\phi(x), \dot{e}=\dot{e}_s\psi(x), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $x\equiv r/r_s$ and $\rho_s\equiv\rho_0(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)$ with $f(1)=g(1)=k(1)=\phi(1)=\psi(1)=1$ and $h(1)=2/(\gamma+1)$.
elements.sent-2411-348: As for the usual ST solution, the dependence of $P_s$, $v_s$, and $\rho_s$ on $r_s$ is determined by the requirement that the $r_s$ dependence drop out of the resulting ordinary differential equations: \begin{equation}P_s =\frac{KE}{2\pi r_s^3}, v_s =\sqrt{\frac{(\gamma+1)KE}{4\pi\rho_0r_s^3}}, r_s =\left[\frac{25(\gamma+1)KE}{16\pi\rho_0}\right]^{1/5}t^{2/5},\end{equation} where $E$ is the energy of the SN explosion and \begin{equation}K =(\gamma-1)\bigg/\int_0^1x^2dx\left[2f+\frac{1}{2}(\gamma+1)gh^2\right],\end{equation} is a dimensionless constant that determines the ratio of thermal to kinetic energy in the SNR.
elements.sent-2411-349: The scaling of all these variables is exactly the same as for the standard ST solution without evaporating clouds, but the value of the constant $K$ and the energy $E$ differs when evaporation and additional cooling are included.
elements.sent-2411-350: Then we aim to solve the functions $f(x)$, $g(x)$, and $h(x)$ given functions $k(x)$, $\phi(x)$, and $\psi(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-351: The resulting ordinary differential equations for $f$, $g$, and $h$ are \begin{equation}(h-x)g'+gh'=-\frac{2gh}{x}+Ak, \frac{2(\gamma-1)}{(\gamma+1)^2}f'+g(h-x)h'=\frac{3}{2}gh+B\phi-Akh,  g(h-x)f'-\gamma f(h-x)g'= 3fg+ \frac{(\gamma+1)^2}{2}g\left(C\psi-B\phi h\right) + Ak\left[\frac{(\gamma+1)^2}{4}gh^2-\gamma f\right] , \end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} A\equiv\dot{\rho}_sr_s/v_s\rho_s, B\equiv\dot{\varrho}_sr_s/v_s^2\rho_s, C\equiv\dot{e}_sr_s/v_s^3\rho_s. \end{aligned}\end{equation} For a similarity solution to exist, $A$, $B$, and $C$ must be constant.
elements.sent-2411-352: This implies that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \dot{\rho}_s\propto v_s/r_s \propto t^{-1}, \dot{\varrho}_s\propto v_s^2/r_s \propto t^{-8/5}, \dot{e}_s\propto v_s^3/r_s \propto t^{-11/5}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} or equivalently for integrated variables, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \dot{M} \propto \dot{\rho}_s r_s^3 \propto v_s r_s^2\propto t^{1/5}, \dot{p} \propto \dot{\varrho}_s r_s^3 \propto v_s^2r_s^2 \propto t^{-2/5}, \dot{E} \propto \dot{e}_s r_s^3 \propto v_s^3r_s^2\propto t^{-1}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Thus if a self-similar solution exists, mass loading, momentum loading, and energy loading will follow a certain scaling of time, i.e., $\dot{\rho _s\propto t^{-1 , \dot{\varrho _s \propto t^{-8/5 , \dot{e _s \propto t^{-11/5}$, and correspondingly, $\dot{M}\propto t^{1/5}$, $\dot{p}\propto t^{-2/5}$, and $\dot{E}\propto t^{-1}$.. { \bibliographystyle{aasjournal \end{CJK*.}}}}}}}}
